fields.description:
## Context
Developers running /dso:remediate on nava-platform projects (flask, nextjs, rails, jekyll-uswds) encounter three classes of bloat that w21-bsnz's bright-line detectors cannot confidently flag: orphaned markdown sections with no inbound cross-references (P-D1), test functions that assert only on mock interactions without checking real behavior (P-T2), and function parameters that are always called with the same literal value at every call site (P-C2). These patterns accumulate unchecked, adding noise to code review, onboarding, and test-suite signal. This epic extends /dso:remediate with static detectors for all three patterns that route every candidate through w21-bsnz's opus blue-team agent for semantic judgment (static detection alone cannot reliably discriminate intent), and extends w21-bsnz's resolver with a refactor_remove_param action so P-C2 CONFIRMs can be auto-applied through Path B.

## Success Criteria
- SC1. Running /dso:remediate --docs on a nava-platform project with a markdown file that contains a section reachable by zero inbound cross-references surfaces that section as a P-D1 candidate through the blue-team routing path.
- SC2. Running /dso:remediate --tests on a nava-platform project with a test function whose assertions target only mock-interaction verification (no assertion with a non-mock left-hand side) surfaces that test as a P-T2 candidate through the blue-team routing path, across Python (python-ast), JS/TS (ast-grep), and Ruby (ast-grep) test files.
- SC3. Running /dso:remediate --code on a nava-platform project with a function whose parameter is passed the same literal value at every call site surfaces that parameter as a P-C2 candidate through the blue-team routing path.
- SC4. When the user selects Path B after a /dso:remediate run that surfaces P-D1, P-T2, or P-C2 candidates with CONFIRM verdicts, the resolver applies the appropriate action — delete_block/delete_file for P-D1, delete_function for P-T2, refactor_remove_param for P-C2 — with the new refactor_remove_param action implemented for Python, JS/TS, and Ruby.
- SC5. Per-template fixtures at tests/fixtures/remediate/<template>/ (flask, nextjs, rails, jekyll-uswds) include expected candidates and expected-false-positive-safe items for each new pattern applicable to the template; automated validation asserts that the blue-team DISMISS rate across fixture candidates is <= 50% per pattern.
- SC6. The existing session-end summary at \$ARTIFACTS_DIR/remediate-summary-<timestamp>.json reflects counts from the new patterns in its existing fields (auto_remediated, blue_team_dismissed, blue_team_needs_human, flagged_for_review, user_resolved, resolver_applied, resolver_no_change) without schema changes.
- SC7. During the sprint, a dogfooding run executes /dso:remediate --docs, --tests, and --code against at least one nava-platform template and captures an attached before/after artifact reporting: (a) candidates surfaced per pattern, (b) blue-team CONFIRM/DISMISS/NEEDS_HUMAN counts per pattern, (c) Path B applications completed for P-C2 CONFIRMs, and (d) the list of noise artifacts actually removed. The artifact is attached to the epic at closure and confirms the capability reduced developer-observable noise in at least one real nava-platform template — not just fixture candidates.

## Out of Scope
- P-D4 (concept drift across doc sections) — evaluated during brainstorm and rejected as infeasible for static detection.
- P-C1 upgrade (env-variable-gated feature flags) — moved to w21-bsnz, where the P-C1 detector already lives.

## Approach
Option A (per-stack static detectors). Each pattern ships as 1-3 language-specific detector scripts registered as distinct {pattern_id, engine, language} entries in w21-bsnz's confidence YAML in the blue-team-only range (>= blue_team_threshold, < auto_threshold). P-D1 uses bash-grep on .md files (permissive by design — code-block link references may reduce orphan-detection sensitivity, which aligns with the <= 50% DISMISS bar; upgrade to a markdown-aware tokenizer at implementation-plan time if fixture data shows insufficient recall). P-T2 and P-C2 use python-ast for Python and ast-grep for JS/TS and Ruby, reusing w21-bsnz's feasibility-validated engine set. A new refactor_remove_param action extends w21-bsnz's bloat-resolver agent via four additive edits: enum definition, action description table row, pattern-specific P-C2 guidance section, cascade-check action list addition. No separate contract/schema file — all changes are in-file. Resolver guidance uses the full "P-C2" prefix consistently to disambiguate from w21-bsnz's bright-line "C2" (unused local variable) pattern.

## Scenario Analysis
- P-C2 literal-value detector misfires on keyword-argument defaults and partial application (critical, runtime): static detection sees "all visible call sites pass X" when some use defaults or functools.partial/.bind, leading refactor_remove_param to strip params external callers still supply.
- refactor_remove_param cascade miss for Ruby keyword arguments and JS/TS destructured options objects (high, runtime): per-language removal semantics may escape a flat cascade-check list.
- ast-grep version/pattern-language drift between JS/TS and Ruby detectors (high, runtime): version mismatch silently matches zero nodes rather than erroring.
- P-T2 mock-only-assertion detector cannot distinguish intentional interaction tests (medium, runtime): legitimate interaction-contract tests may flood blue-team on adapter/integration-heavy stacks (rails, flask).
- Confidence YAML entries in blue-team-only range collide with existing w21-bsnz pattern_ids (high, deployment): duplicate IDs or renamed threshold keys cause silent empty candidate set.
- Per-template fixtures diverge from actual template structure over time (medium, deployment): nava-platform templates evolve independently; fixtures may silently drift.
- refactor_remove_param on fan-in-high files triggers full test-suite gate (high, deployment): widely-called P-C2 targets hit the fan-in threshold; full suite exceeds ~73s tool-timeout ceiling producing spurious exit-144 failures.
- blue_team_threshold >= auto_threshold produces empty blue-team range (high, configuration): merge-conflicted or misconfigured thresholds cause silent routing misbehavior with no validation warning.
- P-D1 --docs flag behavior undefined for zero .md, generated-only .md, or .mdx repos (medium, configuration): bash-grep returns empty; users trust "no bloat" verdict.
- Missing per-language enable/disable switches force all-or-nothing adoption (medium, configuration): teams with confounded Ruby metaprogramming can't opt out per-language, kill adoption.
- remediate-summary-<timestamp>.json field reuse hides new pattern counts from downstream consumers (medium, configuration): consumers that hard-code pattern_ids silently drop new entries — schema-version bump warranted.

## Dependencies
w21-bsnz (Create a process to remediate legacy code) — MUST ship first. This epic extends w21-bsnz's confidence map YAML, blue-team agent, bloat-resolver action enum (adds refactor_remove_param), and session-summary schema. Without w21-bsnz, the new detectors have no routing destination.
